Reconstructing and evaluating that thesis and gap statement is central to any non-trivial review of this section.","R1.2":"A load-bearing internal contradiction is present: the paper simultaneously asserts a 'blind protocol' in which 'the response area exposed no model identifier,' and then describes UI routing badges (a green leaf for local, a blue cloud for cloud) that participants explicitly hovered over before rating. The two framings place different dimensions at load-bearing weight."}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact makes a specific, named HCI contribution claim — trust calibration curves from a blind hybrid deployment. Reconstructing and evaluating that thesis and gap statement is central to any non-trivial review of this section.
rationale.R1.2: A load-bearing internal contradiction is present: the paper simultaneously asserts a 'blind protocol' in which 'the response area exposed no model identifier,' and then describes UI routing badges (a green leaf for local, a blue cloud for cloud) that participants explicitly hovered over before rating. These two commitments are mutually inconsistent; logical consistency is therefore the dominant review concern and failure here invalidates the core causal claim.
rationale.R1.3: The paper's headline conclusion — that trust patterns can be attributed to response content alone, not identity cues — depends entirely on successful blinding. The hover-then-rate evidence it cites (badge hovers at 2.3× dwell time) is itself evidence that participants saw and processed backend-identity cues, creating a direct misalignment between the empirical result and the claim it is meant to support.

parsed.clarification_question: Should this review prioritize methodological and logical soundness of the claims (centering R1.2/R1.3), or writing quality and presentation (centering R1.6)? The two framings place different dimensions at load-bearing weight.
